proposed_hypotheses.text: Each binary feature is associated with a different objective_response rate; specifically I expect at least some of feature_001, feature_005, feature_010, feature_014, feature_021, feature_026 to show a non-zero raw difference in ORR.
Categorical feature_004 (levels 0/1/2) is monotonically associated with objective_response, with higher levels indicating worse response.

analyses.code: for c in binary_cols: stats.chi2_contingency(pd.crosstab(df[c], df['objective_response']))
analyses.result_summary: Chi-squared screen across all binary features. feature_001 (ORR 22.1% vs 16.5%, +5.6pp), feature_014 (23.5% vs 16.7%, +6.7pp), feature_021 (22.0% vs 16.6%, +5.4pp) are the strongest positive associations (each p<1e-50). feature_026 (16.8% vs 19.1%, -2.3pp, p=3.3e-6) and feature_009 (16.6% vs 19.0%, -2.4pp, p=3.5e-5) are negatively associated. feature_010, 005, 016, 025, 019, 015, 018, 008, 006, 032, 030 show no detectable effect (p>0.1).

analyses.code: pd.crosstab(df['feature_004'], df['objective_response']); stats.chi2_contingency(...)
analyses.result_summary: feature_004 ORR by level: 0 -> 23.10% (n=17592), 1 -> 17.43% (n=24971), 2 -> 12.91% (n=7437). Monotonic worsening with increasing level (chi-square p=1.79e-90). Consistent with an ordinal prognostic/risk score.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among continuous features, feature_022, feature_024, feature_020, and feature_027 are negatively associated with objective_response (i.e., higher values predict lower ORR), while feature_029 is positively associated.

analyses.code: for c in cont_cols: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[[c]])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Single-variable logistic regressions: feature_020 beta=-0.0357 p=1.1e-30 (mean 3.44 in responders vs 3.95 in non), feature_024 beta=-0.0049 p=2.4e-16 (58.5 vs 60.3), feature_027 beta=-0.0064 p=6.0e-6 (5.69 vs 6.17), feature_022 beta=-0.0018 p=1.9e-4 (19.3 vs 20.4), feature_029 beta=+0.1175 p=2.9e-7 (3.82 vs 3.80). feature_011 (likely age, range 18-92) is non-significant (p=0.88). feature_034, 028, 007, 031, 013, 033, 003, 035, 023, 002, 017, 012 show no main effect on ORR.

proposed_hypotheses.text: When all features are entered into a multivariable logistic regression for objective_response, feature_001, feature_014, feature_021, feature_004, feature_020, feature_024, feature_029, feature_026, feature_009, feature_027, and feature_022 retain independent significance.

feature_001, feature_014, and feature_021 are essentially independent of each other in the population (no strong pairwise association), so the high-ORR group is not driven by a confounded marker pair.

analyses.code: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[binary_cols + cont_cols])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Full multivariable logistic regression. Significant independent effects: feature_004 beta=-0.36 p=9e-93; feature_014 +0.44 p=1.7e-71; feature_001 +0.37 p=2.4e-57; feature_021 +0.36 p=8.2e-54; feature_020 -0.037 p=2.7e-31; feature_024 -0.0052 p=1.2e-17; feature_029 +0.12 p=1.4e-7; feature_026 -0.17 p=6.1e-7; feature_009 -0.19 p=3.6e-6; feature_027 -0.0065 p=5.8e-6; feature_022 -0.0018 p=1.3e-4; feature_007 +0.0005 p=0.023. All other features are non-significant in the joint model.

analyses.code: for a,b in [('feature_001','feature_014'), ...]: stats.chi2_contingency(pd.crosstab(df[a], df[b]))
analyses.result_summary: Pairwise odds ratios among feature_001, feature_014, feature_021 are essentially 1 (0.986, 0.994, 1.005) with chi-square p=0.48, 0.75, 0.80. They are independently distributed in the cohort. The triple-positive prevalence (2401/50000 = 4.8%) matches the product of marginal probabilities (0.401 x 0.300 x 0.401 = 4.8%), confirming statistical independence.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_001, feature_014, and feature_021 act through pairwise additivity rather than via a higher-order interaction; i.e., having any two of them yields nearly the same ORR as having all three.

analyses.code: ORR by all 8 combinations of (feature_001, feature_014, feature_021)
analyses.result_summary: Cell-wise ORR by (001,014,021): (0,0,0)=17.18%, (0,0,1)=16.21%, (0,1,0)=15.97%, (0,1,1)=15.67%, (1,0,0)=16.45%, (1,0,1)=16.91%, (1,1,0)=15.96%, (1,1,1)=63.31% (n=2401). All seven non-triple cells sit between 15.7% and 17.2%; the triple-positive cell jumps to 63.3%. The hypothesis of pairwise additivity is strongly REFUTED — this is a near-pure 3-way AND interaction.

proposed_hypotheses.text: In a logistic model with all main effects, two-way, and three-way interactions among feature_001, feature_014, feature_021, only the three-way interaction term is significant (with the other terms near zero).

Patients in the triple-positive subgroup (feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1) have an objective_response rate roughly 4-5x higher than the rest of the cohort, with an unadjusted OR ~9 in a logistic model.

analyses.code: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[['feature_001','feature_014','feature_021','x12','x13','x23','x123']])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Full interaction model: main effects feature_001 -0.05 (p=0.16), feature_014 -0.09 (p=0.05), feature_021 -0.07 (p=0.07); two-way interactions x12 +0.05 (p=0.46), x13 +0.10 (p=0.08), x23 +0.05 (p=0.50); three-way interaction x123 +2.13 (p=3.9e-92). The three-way term carries essentially the entire signal — confirms that ALL three biomarkers must be present to see any effect; any subset of two or fewer yields baseline ORR.

analyses.code: df['triple'] = ((df.feature_001==1)&(df.feature_014==1)&(df.feature_021==1)).astype(int); sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[['triple']])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Triple-positive vs rest: ORR 63.31% (n=2401) vs 16.50% (n=47599). Logistic OR=8.73 (beta=2.167), z=49.1, p effectively 0. About 16% of all responders in the cohort come from this 4.8% subgroup.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The conditional effect of each of feature_001, feature_014, feature_021 (given the other two are =1) is symmetric and large; i.e., the three play interchangeable roles in the AND-logic.

analyses.code: Within (014=1 AND 021=1), tabulate ORR by feature_001; analogous for the other two.
analyses.result_summary: Within feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1: feature_001=0 -> 15.67% (n=3624), =1 -> 63.31% (n=2401). Within feature_001=1 AND feature_021=1: feature_014=0 -> 16.91%, =1 -> 63.31%. Within feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1: feature_021=0 -> 15.96%, =1 -> 63.31%. The three features are perfectly symmetric in the AND-interaction: each contributes the same approximately +47 percentage-point lift only when both others are also =1.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The triple-positive effect on objective_response is consistent across feature_004 strata (i.e., the OR is similar regardless of feature_004), even though feature_004 has a strong independent main effect.

analyses.code: stratify by feature_004 in {0,1,2}, compute ORR(triple=1) and ORR(triple=0); test triple x feature_004 interaction in logistic.
analyses.result_summary: Stratified ORR (triple+/triple-): feature_004=0 69.76% vs 20.79% (diff +48.97pp), feature_004=1 61.22% vs 15.18% (+46.03pp), feature_004=2 55.37% vs 10.79% (+44.58pp). Triple x feature_004 interaction term beta=+0.063, p=0.34 (not significant). On the log-odds scale the triple effect is statistically homogeneous across feature_004 strata, but on the absolute risk-difference scale, the lift is largest in the favorable feature_004=0 group.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Within the triple-positive subgroup (n=2401), feature_026 and feature_009 are strong negative predictors of objective_response, even though their marginal effect in the full cohort is small.
Within the triple-positive subgroup, feature_004, feature_020, and feature_029 retain independent associations with response in the same direction as the full-cohort main effects.

analyses.code: sub = df[df.triple==1]; sm.Logit(sub.objective_response, sm.add_constant(sub[all_other_features])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Within-triple logistic regression: feature_026 beta=-2.78 (OR=0.062), p=5.5e-75; feature_009 beta=-2.67 (OR=0.069), p=1.1e-54. These are massive suppressors only inside the triple-positive subset. ORR cells: (triple=1, 026=0, 009=0) -> 78.6% (n=1790); (triple=1, 026=0, 009=1) -> 18.7% (n=235); (triple=1, 026=1, 009=0) -> 18.2% (n=335); (triple=1, 026=1, 009=1) -> 19.5% (n=41). Either suppressor alone is sufficient to abrogate the triple-positive response down to roughly the cohort baseline.

analyses.code: Within triple subset, regress objective_response on remaining features.
analyses.result_summary: Within triple-positive: feature_004 beta=-0.50 p=6.3e-11 (ORR drops with worsening risk: 70%/61%/55% across levels 0/1/2 ignoring suppressors); feature_020 beta=-0.046 p=4.1e-4 (continuous negative trend: tertiles 81%/80%/74%); feature_029 beta=+0.22 p=0.030; feature_033 beta=-0.40 p=0.014. No other within-triple predictor reaches alpha=0.01.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_026 and feature_009 are pure effect modifiers of the triple: among triple-NEGATIVE patients, neither feature has any meaningful association with objective_response, so the suppression is interactive, not main-effect.

A logistic model containing main effects of triple, feature_026, feature_009 and the triple x feature_026 and triple x feature_009 product terms will show no significant main effects of feature_026 or feature_009 but very large negative interaction coefficients.

analyses.code: Within triple==0 stratum, compare ORR by feature_026 and by feature_009.
analyses.result_summary: Within triple=0 (n=47599): feature_026=1 ORR 16.72% vs 026=0 16.46% (diff +0.26pp); feature_009=1 ORR 16.44% vs 009=0 16.51% (diff -0.07pp). Both differences are tiny and non-significant. The negative associations of these two features in the marginal screen are entirely driven by suppression of the triple-positive responders.

analyses.code: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[['triple','feature_026','feature_009','t_x_26','t_x_9']])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Logistic with main effects + triple-by-suppressor interactions: triple +2.90 p=0; feature_026 +0.019 p=0.59; feature_009 -0.005 p=0.90; triple x feature_026 -2.66 p=2.7e-70; triple x feature_009 -2.55 p=4.3e-50. Suppressors have NO main effect; their negative association is entirely an interaction with the triple. These two markers act as inhibitors that selectively block the response generated by the triple-positive phenotype.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The 5-feature 'quint' subgroup defined as (feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1 AND feature_026=0 AND feature_009=0) captures essentially the entire treatment-effect signal: ORR in this subgroup is ~78%, vs ~16-17% outside.
After conditioning on the quint definition, no other binary 3-way subgroup with n>=200 reaches an ORR above 0.40.

analyses.code: df['quint'] = ((feature_001==1)&(feature_014==1)&(feature_021==1)&(feature_026==0)&(feature_009==0)).astype(int); compare ORR; logistic.
analyses.result_summary: Quint-positive: n=1790, ORR=78.60%. Quint-negative: n=48210, ORR=16.53%. Adjusted for feature_004, feature_020, feature_024, feature_029, feature_022, feature_027 in a full logistic model, the quint OR=20.13 (beta=3.00), p effectively 0. The quint subgroup is responsible for 1407 of the cohort's 9375 responders (15%), while comprising only 3.6% of patients.

analyses.code: Enumerate all 3-way products of binary features (excluding the {001,014,021} subset already known), find any with n>=200 and ORR>0.40.
analyses.result_summary: Exhaustive screen of binary 3-way subgroups (n>=200, excluding subsets of {feature_001, feature_014, feature_021}): no other binary triple reaches ORR > 0.40. The only large enriched subgroup in the data is the one already discovered. The next-highest 3-way ORRs (~36-37%) all involve the existing key features (e.g., feature_001 + feature_014 + NOT feature_009).

proposed_hypotheses.text: Within the quint subgroup, feature_004 still substantially modifies response (favorable feature_004=0 -> ~86% ORR; unfavorable feature_004=2 -> ~67% ORR), with a roughly +20 percentage-point spread.
Within the quint subgroup, feature_020 (continuous, range 0-23.6) is negatively associated with response, with high-tertile patients having ~7pp lower ORR than low-tertile patients.

analyses.code: Within quint, ORR by feature_004 levels.
analyses.result_summary: Within quint (n=1790): feature_004=0 ORR 85.90% (n=617), feature_004=1 ORR 77.16% (n=902), feature_004=2 ORR 66.79% (n=271). Within-quint logistic beta for feature_004 = -0.58 (p=6.2e-11). Risk-difference vs the rest of the cohort: 65.1pp, 62.0pp, 55.9pp respectively across levels 0/1/2.

analyses.code: Within quint, ORR by feature_020 tertile.
analyses.result_summary: Within quint, ORR by feature_020 tertile: low (median 0.0) 81.27% (n=614), mid (median 3.2) 79.97% (n=589), high (median 7.9) 74.45% (n=587). Within-quint logistic beta -0.054 per unit (p=2.7e-4). The within-quint feature_020 association is small in absolute terms (~7pp top vs bottom tertile) but consistent in direction.

proposed_hypotheses.text: A 6-feature 'hyper-responder' subgroup defined as (feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1 AND feature_026=0 AND feature_009=0 AND feature_004=0) reaches ORR ~86%, the highest of any clinically interpretable subgroup found in this cohort.
No continuous biomarker except feature_004 and feature_020 shows a within-quint interaction strong enough to define a meaningfully distinct sub-subgroup.

analyses.code: df['hyper'] = ((quint==1)&(feature_004==0)).astype(int); ORR comparisons; OR.
analyses.result_summary: Hyper-responder: n=617, ORR 85.90%. Outside: n=49383, ORR 17.91%. Unadjusted logistic OR=27.9, beta=3.33, p=2.5e-180. By contrast, an unfavorable cell within the quint (quint=1 AND feature_004=2 AND high feature_020 tertile) has ORR ~61%, still well above baseline but markedly lower than the hyper-responder definition.

analyses.code: For each continuous feature c: test triple x c interaction; also within-triple slope.
analyses.result_summary: Triple-by-continuous interaction screen: only feature_024 has an interaction p<0.05 (beta=+0.008, p=5.6e-4), and within-triple slope of feature_024 is non-significant (p=0.34) — i.e., feature_024 has a small main-effect that attenuates inside the triple. All other continuous features (feature_011, feature_022, feature_028, feature_034, feature_007, feature_031, feature_013, feature_033, feature_003, feature_035, feature_023, feature_002, feature_017, feature_027, feature_012) show no triple-by-feature interaction (all p > 0.10).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_011 (likely age, range 18-92) does not modify the triple effect: the triple-positive ORR is similar across the age distribution.

Comparing the triple-positive vs the quint vs the hyper subgroup, ORR strictly increases (63% -> 78% -> 86%) and OR strictly increases (8.7 -> 18.6 -> 27.9), confirming that each suppressor and each prognostic refinement contributes additively to identifying the high-response phenotype.

analyses.code: Compute triple x feature_011 interaction and within-triple feature_011 slope.
analyses.result_summary: Triple x feature_011 interaction: beta=-0.0036, p=0.38. Within-triple feature_011 slope: beta=-0.0032, p=0.41. feature_011 (likely age) is not a meaningful effect modifier of the triple — the triple-positive phenotype responds similarly across the age range, and feature_011 also has no detectable main effect on objective_response in the full cohort (p=0.88).

analyses.code: Compute ORR and unadjusted OR vs the rest of cohort for triple, quint, hyper definitions.
analyses.result_summary: Single-marker (feature_001 alone): ORR 22.1%, OR 1.43. Triple (001+014+021): ORR 63.3%, OR 8.7. Quint (triple AND 026=0 AND 009=0): ORR 78.6%, OR 18.6. Hyper (quint AND feature_004=0): ORR 85.9%, OR 27.9. The OR roughly doubles at each refinement, demonstrating that each successive predicate refines the high-response phenotype.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Treating triple-positivity as the implicit 'treatment' (the only feature combination producing material response heterogeneity), the most complete subgroup-defining heterogeneity statement for objective_response is: triple-positive patients (feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1) have a large positive treatment effect that is suppressed if feature_026=1 OR feature_009=1, and that is most pronounced in the favorable feature_004=0 stratum and at lower values of feature_020.

analyses.code: Synthesize subgroup ORRs across the 6-feature lattice.
analyses.result_summary: Final subgroup synthesis: ORR(triple=1, feature_026=0, feature_009=0, feature_004=0) = 85.9% (n=617); ORR(triple=1, feature_026=0, feature_009=0, feature_004 in {1,2}) = 75.9% (n=1173); ORR(triple=1 with EITHER feature_026=1 OR feature_009=1) = 18.6% (n=611, indistinguishable from triple-negative baseline ~16.5%); ORR(triple=0, any other features) = 16.5% (n=47599). The subgroup definition with all five binary predicates and the favorable feature_004 level provides a 5-fold enrichment over baseline. Lower feature_020 within the subgroup adds a further ~7pp ORR. No other features modify response in the high-response subgroup.
